tools.name: configuration_view
tools.description: Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML

minified.description: Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)

tools.name: events_list
tools.description: List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter events by field values (e.g. 'type=Warning', 'involvedObject.name=my-pod'). Supported fields: involvedObject.kind, involvedObject.name, involvedObject.namespace, involvedObject.uid, involvedObject.apiVersion, involvedObject.resourceVersion, involvedObject.fieldPath, reason, reportingComponent, source, type. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces

tools.name: namespaces_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter namespaces by field values (e.g. 'metadata.name=default', 'status.phase=Active'). Supported fields: metadata.name, status.phase. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

tools.name: nodes_log
tools.description: Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet

name.description: Name of the node to get logs from

query.description: query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")

tailLines.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)

tools.name: nodes_stats_summary
tools.description: Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics

name.description: Name of the node to get stats from

tools.name: nodes_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)

tools.name: pods_delete
tools.description: Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

name.description: Name of the Pod to delete

namespace.description: Namespace to delete the Pod from

tools.name: pods_exec
tools.description: Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command

command.description: Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]

container.description: Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod where the command will be executed

namespace.description: Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed

tools.name: pods_get
tools.description: Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod from

tools.name: pods_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_list_in_namespace
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the specified namespace in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_log
tools.description: Get the logs of a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

container.description: Name of the Pod container to get the logs from (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the logs from

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod logs from

tail.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, default: 100)

tools.name: pods_run
tools.description: Run a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided container image and optional name

image.description: Container Image to run in the Pod

name.description: Name of the Pod (Optional, random name if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to run the Pod in

port.description: TCP/IP port to expose from the Pod container (Optional, no port exposed if not provided)

tools.name: pods_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Pods in the all namespaces, the provided namespace, or the current namespace

all_namespaces.description: If true, list the resource consumption for all Pods in all namespaces. If false, list the resource consumption for Pods in the provided namespace or the current namespace

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Pods in the namespace if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pods resource consumption from (Optional, current namespace if not provided and all_namespaces is false)

tools.name: resources_create_or_update
tools.description:
Create or update a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing a YAML or JSON representation of the resource
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

resource.description: A JSON or YAML containing a representation of the Kubernetes resource. Should include top-level fields such as apiVersion,kind,metadata, and spec

tools.name: resources_delete
tools.description:
Delete a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

gracePeriodSeconds.description: Optional duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. Value must be non-negative integer. The value zero indicates delete immediately. If this value is nil, the default grace period for the specified type will be used

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to delete the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will delete resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_get
tools.description:
Get a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_list
tools.description:
List Kubernetes resources and objects in the current cluster by providing their apiVersion and kind and optionally the namespace and label selector
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resources (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter resources by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'metadata.name=myresource'). Supported fields vary by resource type. For Pods: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

kind.description: kind of the resources (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the resources by label

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resources from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will list resources from all namespaces

tools.name: resources_scale
tools.description: Get or update the scale of a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, name, and optionally the namespace. If the scale is set in the tool call, the scale will be updated to that value. Always returns the current scale of the resource

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are apps/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: StatefulSet, Deployment)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to get/update the namespaced resource scale from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get/update resource scale from configured namespace

scale.description: Optional scale to update the resources scale to. If not provided, will return the current scale of the resource, and not update it

results.taskOutput:
Done. Here’s what I set up and verified:

What I deployed
- Canary Deployment: engine-v2-1 (namespace: canary-deployment-ns)
  - Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Replicas: 2
  - Labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1
  - Container: rec-engine, containerPort: 80 (matching the stable deployment’s port and security configuration)
- Service update: recommendation-engine
  - Selector changed to app=recommendation-engine (removed version constraint) so it targets both stable and canary
  - Port config left as-is: port 80 -> targetPort 8080

Verification
- Deployments
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 ready
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 ready
- Pods (all Running)
  - v2.0: engine-v2-0-7c5c5d9f94-9cznm, engine-v2-0-7c5c5d9f94-tb9s9
  - v2.1: engine-v2-1-757b7f7b64-86wph, engine-v2-1-757b7f7b64-9krp4
- Service endpoints now include all four pods, confirming traffic will be balanced across both deployments (approximately 50/50 given equal replica counts).

Notes
- Kubernetes will distribute connections roughly evenly across endpoints; with 2 pods in each deployment, this yields about a 50/50 split.
- The Service continues to targetPort 8080 (as before). The nginx-unprivileged image listens on 8080 by default; the containerPort declaration at 80 is informational and matches the stable setup.

Roll back or adjust
- To revert to 100% stable: set the Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.0, or scale down/delete engine-v2-1.
- To change the canary weight: adjust replica counts (e.g., stable=3, canary=1 ~25%).

Steps.message:
namespace/canary-deployment-ns created
deployment.apps/engine-v2-0 created
service/recommendation-engine created

Done. Here’s what I set up and verified:

What I deployed
- Canary Deployment: engine-v2-1 (namespace: canary-deployment-ns)
  - Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Replicas: 2
  - Labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1
  - Container: rec-engine, containerPort: 80 (matching the stable deployment’s port and security configuration)
- Service update: recommendation-engine
  - Selector changed to app=recommendation-engine (removed version constraint) so it targets both stable and canary
  - Port config left as-is: port 80 -> targetPort 8080

Verification
- Deployments
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 ready
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 ready
- Pods (all Running)
  - v2.0: engine-v2-0-7c5c5d9f94-9cznm, engine-v2-0-7c5c5d9f94-tb9s9
  - v2.1: engine-v2-1-757b7f7b64-86wph, engine-v2-1-757b7f7b64-9krp4
- Service endpoints now include all four pods, confirming traffic will be balanced across both deployments (approximately 50/50 given equal replica counts).

Notes
- Kubernetes will distribute connections roughly evenly across endpoints; with 2 pods in each deployment, this yields about a 50/50 split.
- The Service continues to targetPort 8080 (as before). The nginx-unprivileged image listens on 8080 by default; the containerPort declaration at 80 is informational and matches the stable setup.

Roll back or adjust
- To revert to 100% stable: set the Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.0, or scale down/delete engine-v2-1.
- To change the canary weight: adjust replica counts (e.g., stable=3, canary=1 ~25%).

outputSteps.content:
Done. Here’s what I set up and verified:

What I deployed
- Canary Deployment: engine-v2-1 (namespace: canary-deployment-ns)
  - Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Replicas: 2
  - Labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1
  - Container: rec-engine, containerPort: 80 (matching the stable deployment’s port and security configuration)
- Service update: recommendation-engine
  - Selector changed to app=recommendation-engine (removed version constraint) so it targets both stable and canary
  - Port config left as-is: port 80 -> targetPort 8080

Verification
- Deployments
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 ready
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 ready
- Pods (all Running)
  - v2.0: engine-v2-0-7c5c5d9f94-9cznm, engine-v2-0-7c5c5d9f94-tb9s9
  - v2.1: engine-v2-1-757b7f7b64-86wph, engine-v2-1-757b7f7b64-9krp4
- Service endpoints now include all four pods, confirming traffic will be balanced across both deployments (approximately 50/50 given equal replica counts).

Notes
- Kubernetes will distribute connections roughly evenly across endpoints; with 2 pods in each deployment, this yields about a 50/50 split.
- The Service continues to targetPort 8080 (as before). The nginx-unprivileged image listens on 8080 by default; the containerPort declaration at 80 is informational and matches the stable setup.

Roll back or adjust
- To revert to 100% stable: set the Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.0, or scale down/delete engine-v2-1.
- To change the canary weight: adjust replica counts (e.g., stable=3, canary=1 ~25%).

results.taskOutput:
Short answer: the pod is fine; the app inside it isn’t.

What I see:
- Pod status: Running and Ready. No Kubernetes warnings/errors in events. Image pulled and container started normally.
- Logs from calc-app-pod show intermittent “division by zero” errors:
  - e.g., “Run 5/9/10/17/18 failed with error: division by zero”

Conclusion:
- This isn’t a Kubernetes issue. Your Python app (python:3.9-slim-buster running /etc/config/calc-app.py from the calc-app-map ConfigMap) is occasionally dividing by zero. Fix the app logic or sanitize inputs to avoid zero denominators, or catch ZeroDivisionError and handle it.

If helpful, I can pull and review the calc-app-map ConfigMap to point to the exact code path causing the error.

outputSteps.content:
Short answer: the pod is fine; the app inside it isn’t.

What I see:
- Pod status: Running and Ready. No Kubernetes warnings/errors in events. Image pulled and container started normally.
- Logs from calc-app-pod show intermittent “division by zero” errors:
  - e.g., “Run 5/9/10/17/18 failed with error: division by zero”

Conclusion:
- This isn’t a Kubernetes issue. Your Python app (python:3.9-slim-buster running /etc/config/calc-app.py from the calc-app-map ConfigMap) is occasionally dividing by zero. Fix the app logic or sanitize inputs to avoid zero denominators, or catch ZeroDivisionError and handle it.

If helpful, I can pull and review the calc-app-map ConfigMap to point to the exact code path causing the error.

Steps.message: Pass under CONTAINS: The model response explicitly identifies “division by zero” errors in the app logs, which is the entirety of the core information in the reference answer. Extra contextual details about the pod status and app environment are non-contradictory and acceptable. Therefore, it semantically includes the reference’s meaning.

results.taskOutput:
I found the issue and fixed it.

What was wrong:
- The Deployment app in namespace crashloop-test was overriding the container entrypoint with: command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"] and args: ["nonexistent_command"], causing CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed:
- Updated the Deployment to run nginx in the foreground:
  - command: ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
  - Kept image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged and exposed containerPort 8080.

Current state:
- Deployment app is Available and Progressing.
- One Pod is Running and Ready: app-74b5447b6c-mdjts (1/1).
- Deployment status shows minimum availability satisfied.

If you prefer to rely on the image’s default entrypoint instead, I can remove the explicit command entirely.

outputSteps.content:
I found the issue and fixed it.

What was wrong:
- The Deployment app in namespace crashloop-test was overriding the container entrypoint with: command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"] and args: ["nonexistent_command"], causing CrashLoopBackOff.

What I changed:
- Updated the Deployment to run nginx in the foreground:
  - command: ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
  - Kept image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged and exposed containerPort 8080.

Current state:
- Deployment app is Available and Progressing.
- One Pod is Running and Ready: app-74b5447b6c-mdjts (1/1).
- Deployment status shows minimum availability satisfied.

If you prefer to rely on the image’s default entrypoint instead, I can remove the explicit command entirely.

results.taskOutput:
Fixed.

- Cause: The Deployment debug/app was failing with ImagePullBackOff due to an invalid image tag (quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag).
- Action taken: Updated the Deployment to use a valid image tag: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest.
- Result: Deployment is now Available with 1/1 replicas ready. Current Pod is running: app-766ff8b47b-97f8l.

If you prefer a pinned version for reproducibility, tell me the desired tag (or digest) and I’ll update the Deployment accordingly.

Steps.message:
Fixed.

- Cause: The Deployment debug/app was failing with ImagePullBackOff due to an invalid image tag (quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag).
- Action taken: Updated the Deployment to use a valid image tag: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest.
- Result: Deployment is now Available with 1/1 replicas ready. Current Pod is running: app-766ff8b47b-97f8l.

If you prefer a pinned version for reproducibility, tell me the desired tag (or digest) and I’ll update the Deployment accordingly.

outputSteps.content:
Fixed.

- Cause: The Deployment debug/app was failing with ImagePullBackOff due to an invalid image tag (quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag).
- Action taken: Updated the Deployment to use a valid image tag: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest.
- Result: Deployment is now Available with 1/1 replicas ready. Current Pod is running: app-766ff8b47b-97f8l.

If you prefer a pinned version for reproducibility, tell me the desired tag (or digest) and I’ll update the Deployment accordingly.

Steps.message:
deployment.apps/app condition met
Pod is stable. Verification successful.

results.taskOutput:
I’ve fixed it. Summary of what I did and the current state:

What was wrong
- The pod was Pending because its PVC homepage-pvc (storageClassName: sc) was unbound. The StorageClass sc didn’t exist, so no volume could be provisioned.

What I changed
- Created a StorageClass named sc using the local-path provisioner (rancher.io/local-path).
- Installed and started the local-path-provisioner in namespace local-path-storage so dynamic provisioning works on this cluster.

Current status
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (1Gi) via StorageClass sc to PV pvc-2d0ed3f5-8d42-4ed6-9735-0c9a79129fd6.
- Pod homepage-pod (homepage-ns): Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane. Container nginx is ready.

If you prefer to use the existing default StorageClass standard instead of sc, I can update the PVC and clean up the custom class.

arguments.resource:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner-service-account
  namespace: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner-role
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes", "persistentvolumeclaims", "configmaps", "events", "endpoints"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["persistentvolumes"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["storage.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["storageclasses"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner-bind
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: local-path-provisioner-role
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: local-path-provisioner-service-account
  namespace: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: leader-locking-local-path-provisioner
  namespace: local-path-storage
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["endpoints"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: leader-locking-local-path-provisioner
  namespace: local-path-storage
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: leader-locking-local-path-provisioner
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: local-path-provisioner-service-account
  namespace: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: local-path-config
  namespace: local-path-storage
  labels:
    app: local-path-provisioner
    name: local-path-config
  annotations:
    version: v0.0.24
data:
  config.json: |
    {
        "nodePathMap":[
        {
            "node":"DEFAULT_PATH_FOR_NON_LISTED_NODES",
            "paths":["/opt/local-path-provisioner"]
        }]
    }
  setup: |
    #!/bin/sh
    set -eu
    mkdir -m 0777 -p "$VOL_DIR"
    chown -R 0:0 "$VOL_DIR"
  teardown: |
    #!/bin/sh
    set -eu
    rm -rf "$VOL_DIR"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner
  namespace: local-path-storage
  labels:
    app: local-path-provisioner
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: local-path-provisioner
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: local-path-provisioner
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: local-path-provisioner-service-account
      containers:
      - name: local-path-provisioner
        image: rancher/local-path-provisioner:v0.0.24
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        command:
        - local-path-provisioner
        - --debug
        - start
        - --helper-image
        - busybox:1.36
        - --config
        - /etc/config/config.json
        env:
        - name: PROVISIONER_NAME
          value: rancher.io/local-path
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/config/
        - name: local-path
          mountPath: /opt/local-path-provisioner
      volumes:
      - name: config-volume
        configMap:
          name: local-path-config
      - name: local-path
        hostPath:
          path: /opt/local-path-provisioner
          type: DirectoryOrCreate

Steps.message:
namespace/homepage-ns created
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc created
pod/homepage-pod created

I’ve fixed it. Summary of what I did and the current state:

What was wrong
- The pod was Pending because its PVC homepage-pvc (storageClassName: sc) was unbound. The StorageClass sc didn’t exist, so no volume could be provisioned.

What I changed
- Created a StorageClass named sc using the local-path provisioner (rancher.io/local-path).
- Installed and started the local-path-provisioner in namespace local-path-storage so dynamic provisioning works on this cluster.

Current status
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (1Gi) via StorageClass sc to PV pvc-2d0ed3f5-8d42-4ed6-9735-0c9a79129fd6.
- Pod homepage-pod (homepage-ns): Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane. Container nginx is ready.

If you prefer to use the existing default StorageClass standard instead of sc, I can update the PVC and clean up the custom class.

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner-service-account
  namespace: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner-role
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes", "persistentvolumeclaims", "configmaps", "events", "endpoints"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["persistentvolumes"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["storage.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["storageclasses"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner-bind
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: local-path-provisioner-role
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: local-path-provisioner-service-account
  namespace: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: leader-locking-local-path-provisioner
  namespace: local-path-storage
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["endpoints"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: leader-locking-local-path-provisioner
  namespace: local-path-storage
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: leader-locking-local-path-provisioner
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: local-path-provisioner-service-account
  namespace: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: local-path-config
  namespace: local-path-storage
  labels:
    app: local-path-provisioner
    name: local-path-config
  annotations:
    version: v0.0.24
data:
  config.json: |
    {
        "nodePathMap":[
        {
            "node":"DEFAULT_PATH_FOR_NON_LISTED_NODES",
            "paths":["/opt/local-path-provisioner"]
        }]
    }
  setup: |
    #!/bin/sh
    set -eu
    mkdir -m 0777 -p "$VOL_DIR"
    chown -R 0:0 "$VOL_DIR"
  teardown: |
    #!/bin/sh
    set -eu
    rm -rf "$VOL_DIR"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner
  namespace: local-path-storage
  labels:
    app: local-path-provisioner
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: local-path-provisioner
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: local-path-provisioner
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: local-path-provisioner-service-account
      containers:
      - name: local-path-provisioner
        image: rancher/local-path-provisioner:v0.0.24
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        command:
        - local-path-provisioner
        - --debug
        - start
        - --helper-image
        - busybox:1.36
        - --config
        - /etc/config/config.json
        env:
        - name: PROVISIONER_NAME
          value: rancher.io/local-path
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/config/
        - name: local-path
          mountPath: /opt/local-path-provisioner
      volumes:
      - name: config-volume
        configMap:
          name: local-path-config
      - name: local-path
        hostPath:
          path: /opt/local-path-provisioner
          type: DirectoryOrCreate

{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"# The following events (YAML format) were found:
- InvolvedObject:
    Kind: PersistentVolumeClaim

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner-service-account
  namespace: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner-role
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes", "persistentvolumeclaims", "configmaps", "events", "endpoints"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["persistentvolumes"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["storage.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["storageclasses"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner-bind
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: local-path-provisioner-role
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: local-path-provisioner-service-account
  namespace: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: leader-locking-local-path-provisioner
  namespace: local-path-storage
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["endpoints"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: leader-locking-local-path-provisioner
  namespace: local-path-storage
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: leader-locking-local-path-provisioner
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: local-path-provisioner-service-account
  namespace: local-path-storage
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: local-path-config
  namespace: local-path-storage
  labels:
    app: local-path-provisioner
    name: local-path-config
  annotations:
    version: v0.0.24
data:
  config.json: |
    {
        "nodePathMap":[
        {
            "node":"DEFAULT_PATH_FOR_NON_LISTED_NODES",
            "paths":["/opt/local-path-provisioner"]
        }]
    }
  setup: |
    #!/bin/sh
    set -eu
    mkdir -m 0777 -p "$VOL_DIR"
    chown -R 0:0 "$VOL_DIR"
  teardown: |
    #!/bin/sh
    set -eu
    rm -rf "$VOL_DIR"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: local-path-provisioner
  namespace: local-path-storage
  labels:
    app: local-path-provisioner
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: local-path-provisioner
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: local-path-provisioner
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: local-path-provisioner-service-account
      containers:
      - name: local-path-provisioner
        image: rancher/local-path-provisioner:v0.0.24
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        command:
        - local-path-provisioner
        - --debug
        - start
        - --helper-image
        - busybox:1.36
        - --config
        - /etc/config/config.json
        env:
        - name: PROVISIONER_NAME
          value: rancher.io/local-path
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/config/
        - name: local-path
          mountPath: /opt/local-path-provisioner
      volumes:
      - name: config-volume
        configMap:
          name: local-path-config
      - name: local-path
        hostPath:
          path: /opt/local-path-provisioner
          type: DirectoryOrCreate

outputSteps.content:
I’ve fixed it. Summary of what I did and the current state:

What was wrong
- The pod was Pending because its PVC homepage-pvc (storageClassName: sc) was unbound. The StorageClass sc didn’t exist, so no volume could be provisioned.

What I changed
- Created a StorageClass named sc using the local-path provisioner (rancher.io/local-path).
- Installed and started the local-path-provisioner in namespace local-path-storage so dynamic provisioning works on this cluster.

Current status
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (1Gi) via StorageClass sc to PV pvc-2d0ed3f5-8d42-4ed6-9735-0c9a79129fd6.
- Pod homepage-pod (homepage-ns): Running and Ready on node mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane. Container nginx is ready.

If you prefer to use the existing default StorageClass standard instead of sc, I can update the PVC and clean up the custom class.

Steps.message:
Starting verification for fix-pending-pods...
ℹWaiting for PVC 'homepage-pvc' to be 'Bound'...
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc condition met
'homepage-pvc' is Bound. Verifying that desired state is realized...
Waiting for Pod 'homepage-pod' to be 'Ready'...
pod/homepage-pod condition met
Pod 'homepage-pod' is Ready. Verification successful for .

results.taskOutput:
I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The container image (nginx-unprivileged) listens on port 8080, but the Deployment’s liveness/readiness probes were checking port 80 on non-existent paths (/get_status and /is_ready).
- Because of that, the pod never became Ready and the Service had no endpoints.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment probes to GET / on port 8080.
- Updated the Service to targetPort 8080.
- The Deployment rolled out a new pod.

Current status
- Pod: 1/1 Ready (webapp-686744f9b7-hls8l).
- Service endpoints: 10.244.0.33:8080 registered.
- The app is now healthy and serving traffic.

How to reach it
- In-cluster: http://webapp.orders.svc.cluster.local/
- From your machine: run kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80, then visit http://localhost:8080/

If you want it exposed externally, I can set up an Ingress or a NodePort.

kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: webapp
  namespace: orders
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: webapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: webapp

        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
          name: http
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10

Steps.message:
namespace/orders created
deployment.apps/webapp created
service/webapp created
Waiting for pod to start and begin failing health checks...
deployment.apps/webapp condition met
Setup successful: Deployment is no longer available.

I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The container image (nginx-unprivileged) listens on port 8080, but the Deployment’s liveness/readiness probes were checking port 80 on non-existent paths (/get_status and /is_ready).
- Because of that, the pod never became Ready and the Service had no endpoints.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment probes to GET / on port 8080.
- Updated the Service to targetPort 8080.
- The Deployment rolled out a new pod.

Current status
- Pod: 1/1 Ready (webapp-686744f9b7-hls8l).
- Service endpoints: 10.244.0.33:8080 registered.
- The app is now healthy and serving traffic.

How to reach it
- In-cluster: http://webapp.orders.svc.cluster.local/
- From your machine: run kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80, then visit http://localhost:8080/

If you want it exposed externally, I can set up an Ingress or a NodePort.

outputSteps.content:
I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The container image (nginx-unprivileged) listens on port 8080, but the Deployment’s liveness/readiness probes were checking port 80 on non-existent paths (/get_status and /is_ready).
- Because of that, the pod never became Ready and the Service had no endpoints.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment probes to GET / on port 8080.
- Updated the Service to targetPort 8080.
- The Deployment rolled out a new pod.

Current status
- Pod: 1/1 Ready (webapp-686744f9b7-hls8l).
- Service endpoints: 10.244.0.33:8080 registered.
- The app is now healthy and serving traffic.

How to reach it
- In-cluster: http://webapp.orders.svc.cluster.local/
- From your machine: run kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80, then visit http://localhost:8080/

If you want it exposed externally, I can set up an Ingress or a NodePort.

Steps.message:
Checking if the pod is running and ready...
pod/webapp-686744f9b7-hls8l condition met
Success: Pod is now Ready
Current liveness probe path: /
Current readiness probe path: /
Success: Both probe paths have been fixed
Success: Pod is stable with acceptable number of restarts

results.taskOutput:
Root cause
- The Role bound to your pod-reader service account only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant permissions on core/v1 pods, so listing pods failed.

What I changed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to include:
  - apiGroup: "" (core), resources: pods, verbs: get, list, watch
  - Kept existing permission to list apps/deployments

Current binding
- RoleBinding pod-reader-binding correctly binds Role/pod-reader-role to ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader.

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --namespace simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader list pods
- kubectl get pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

If you also need other read actions (e.g., get pod logs), let me know and I’ll add them.

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader-role
  namespace: simple-rbac-setup
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["list"]

Steps.message:
namespace/simple-rbac-setup created
serviceaccount/pod-reader created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-role created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-binding created

Root cause
- The Role bound to your pod-reader service account only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant permissions on core/v1 pods, so listing pods failed.

What I changed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to include:
  - apiGroup: "" (core), resources: pods, verbs: get, list, watch
  - Kept existing permission to list apps/deployments

Current binding
- RoleBinding pod-reader-binding correctly binds Role/pod-reader-role to ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader.

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --namespace simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader list pods
- kubectl get pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

If you also need other read actions (e.g., get pod logs), let me know and I’ll add them.

outputSteps.content:
Root cause
- The Role bound to your pod-reader service account only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant permissions on core/v1 pods, so listing pods failed.

What I changed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to include:
  - apiGroup: "" (core), resources: pods, verbs: get, list, watch
  - Kept existing permission to list apps/deployments

Current binding
- RoleBinding pod-reader-binding correctly binds Role/pod-reader-role to ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader.

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --namespace simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader list pods
- kubectl get pods -n simple-rbac-setup --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader

If you also need other read actions (e.g., get pod logs), let me know and I’ll add them.

Steps.message: Verification successful: RBAC role correctly reconfigured.

results.taskOutput:
Diagnosis
- The Service selector didn’t match the Pods.
  - Deployment/Pods are labeled app=nginx.
  - Service nginx was selecting app=web, so it had no endpoints.
- The container image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on port 8080, but the Service was targeting port 80. Even with a correct selector, traffic would fail.

What I changed
- Updated the Service in namespace web:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports: port 80 -> targetPort 8080 (named “http”)

Verification
- Before: Endpoints for Service nginx were empty.
- After: Endpoints show the Pod IP and port:
  - web/endpoints nginx -> 10.244.0.34:8080
- The Service should now be reachable inside the cluster at:
  - DNS: http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local/
  - ClusterIP: http://10.96.132.232/

How you can double-check from your shell
- kubectl -n web get svc nginx,endpoints nginx
- kubectl run -n web tmp --rm -it --restart=Never --image=curlimages/curl -- curl -sI http://nginx

Note on external access
- The Service type is ClusterIP (internal only). If you need access from outside the cluster, choose one:
  - NodePort: kubectl -n web patch svc nginx -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort","ports":[{"port":80,"targetPort":8080,"nodePort":30080}]}}'
  - Ingress (recommended with an Ingress Controller): create an Ingress that routes host/path to Service nginx:80.

Tell me if you want me to expose it via NodePort or set up an Ingress.

failed to exec in pod curl-test in namespace web: Internal error occurred: unable to upgrade connection: container not found ("curl-test")

command: curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local/ ; getent hosts nginx ; nslookup nginx || true ; nc -vz -w2 nginx 80 || true ; echo DONE
arguments.name: curl-test

Steps.message:
namespace/web created
deployment.apps/nginx created
service/nginx created

Diagnosis
- The Service selector didn’t match the Pods.
  - Deployment/Pods are labeled app=nginx.
  - Service nginx was selecting app=web, so it had no endpoints.
- The container image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on port 8080, but the Service was targeting port 80. Even with a correct selector, traffic would fail.

What I changed
- Updated the Service in namespace web:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports: port 80 -> targetPort 8080 (named “http”)

Verification
- Before: Endpoints for Service nginx were empty.
- After: Endpoints show the Pod IP and port:
  - web/endpoints nginx -> 10.244.0.34:8080
- The Service should now be reachable inside the cluster at:
  - DNS: http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local/
  - ClusterIP: http://10.96.132.232/

How you can double-check from your shell
- kubectl -n web get svc nginx,endpoints nginx
- kubectl run -n web tmp --rm -it --restart=Never --image=curlimages/curl -- curl -sI http://nginx

Note on external access
- The Service type is ClusterIP (internal only). If you need access from outside the cluster, choose one:
  - NodePort: kubectl -n web patch svc nginx -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort","ports":[{"port":80,"targetPort":8080,"nodePort":30080}]}}'
  - Ingress (recommended with an Ingress Controller): create an Ingress that routes host/path to Service nginx:80.

Tell me if you want me to expose it via NodePort or set up an Ingress.

outputSteps.content:
Diagnosis
- The Service selector didn’t match the Pods.
  - Deployment/Pods are labeled app=nginx.
  - Service nginx was selecting app=web, so it had no endpoints.
- The container image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on port 8080, but the Service was targeting port 80. Even with a correct selector, traffic would fail.

What I changed
- Updated the Service in namespace web:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports: port 80 -> targetPort 8080 (named “http”)

Verification
- Before: Endpoints for Service nginx were empty.
- After: Endpoints show the Pod IP and port:
  - web/endpoints nginx -> 10.244.0.34:8080
- The Service should now be reachable inside the cluster at:
  - DNS: http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local/
  - ClusterIP: http://10.96.132.232/

How you can double-check from your shell
- kubectl -n web get svc nginx,endpoints nginx
- kubectl run -n web tmp --rm -it --restart=Never --image=curlimages/curl -- curl -sI http://nginx

Note on external access
- The Service type is ClusterIP (internal only). If you need access from outside the cluster, choose one:
  - NodePort: kubectl -n web patch svc nginx -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort","ports":[{"port":80,"targetPort":8080,"nodePort":30080}]}}'
  - Ingress (recommended with an Ingress Controller): create an Ingress that routes host/path to Service nginx:80.

Tell me if you want me to expose it via NodePort or set up an Ingress.

pod "test-connection" deleted from web namespace

results.taskOutput:
Diagnosis
- Pods weren’t running because the Deployment had a nodeSelector that doesn’t match any node:
  - nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu
  - Events showed FailedScheduling: “1 node(s) didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector.”
  - With no pods, the Service had no endpoints, so connections to web-app-service were refused.

- Additionally, the image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on 8080 by default. The Service was targeting port 80, which would have caused connection issues even after pods started if targetPort didn’t match.

What I changed
- Removed the mismatched nodeSelector by recreating the Deployment without it, and kept replicas=2.
- Standardized on the unprivileged NGINX port:
  - Container now exposes containerPort: 8080
  - Service updated to targetPort: 8080 (Service port remains 80)

Current state
- Pods:
  - web-app-deployment-9ff44f8b6-f49r5 — Running (10.244.0.38)
  - web-app-deployment-9ff44f8b6-w9sl5 — Running (10.244.0.37)
- Service endpoints now populated:
  - web-app-service → 10.244.0.37:8080, 10.244.0.38:8080

This should resolve the connection refused errors to web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local.

Notes and recommendations
- If you intended to schedule on special “GPU” nodes, label those nodes with environment=production-gpu (or adjust the selector). Otherwise, keep the selector removed so pods can schedule anywhere.
- Consider adding liveness/readiness probes to the Deployment to ensure stable rollouts and endpoint health.
- Keep avoiding the :latest tag for reproducibility; we’re using a pinned version.

Want me to add health probes or restore the app to listen on port 80 instead (and switch Service targetPort back to 80)?

Steps.message:
namespace/webshop-frontend created
service/web-app-service created
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment created
Waiting for resources to be created...
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment condition met
Warning: v1 Endpoints is deprecated in v1.33+; use discovery.k8s.io/v1 EndpointSlice
Setup successful: Service has no endpoints as expected

Diagnosis
- Pods weren’t running because the Deployment had a nodeSelector that doesn’t match any node:
  - nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu
  - Events showed FailedScheduling: “1 node(s) didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector.”
  - With no pods, the Service had no endpoints, so connections to web-app-service were refused.

- Additionally, the image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on 8080 by default. The Service was targeting port 80, which would have caused connection issues even after pods started if targetPort didn’t match.

What I changed
- Removed the mismatched nodeSelector by recreating the Deployment without it, and kept replicas=2.
- Standardized on the unprivileged NGINX port:
  - Container now exposes containerPort: 8080
  - Service updated to targetPort: 8080 (Service port remains 80)

Current state
- Pods:
  - web-app-deployment-9ff44f8b6-f49r5 — Running (10.244.0.38)
  - web-app-deployment-9ff44f8b6-w9sl5 — Running (10.244.0.37)
- Service endpoints now populated:
  - web-app-service → 10.244.0.37:8080, 10.244.0.38:8080

This should resolve the connection refused errors to web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local.

Notes and recommendations
- If you intended to schedule on special “GPU” nodes, label those nodes with environment=production-gpu (or adjust the selector). Otherwise, keep the selector removed so pods can schedule anywhere.
- Consider adding liveness/readiness probes to the Deployment to ensure stable rollouts and endpoint health.
- Keep avoiding the :latest tag for reproducibility; we’re using a pinned version.

Want me to add health probes or restore the app to listen on port 80 instead (and switch Service targetPort back to 80)?

outputSteps.content:
Diagnosis
- Pods weren’t running because the Deployment had a nodeSelector that doesn’t match any node:
  - nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu
  - Events showed FailedScheduling: “1 node(s) didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector.”
  - With no pods, the Service had no endpoints, so connections to web-app-service were refused.

- Additionally, the image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on 8080 by default. The Service was targeting port 80, which would have caused connection issues even after pods started if targetPort didn’t match.

What I changed
- Removed the mismatched nodeSelector by recreating the Deployment without it, and kept replicas=2.
- Standardized on the unprivileged NGINX port:
  - Container now exposes containerPort: 8080
  - Service updated to targetPort: 8080 (Service port remains 80)

Current state
- Pods:
  - web-app-deployment-9ff44f8b6-f49r5 — Running (10.244.0.38)
  - web-app-deployment-9ff44f8b6-w9sl5 — Running (10.244.0.37)
- Service endpoints now populated:
  - web-app-service → 10.244.0.37:8080, 10.244.0.38:8080

This should resolve the connection refused errors to web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local.

Notes and recommendations
- If you intended to schedule on special “GPU” nodes, label those nodes with environment=production-gpu (or adjust the selector). Otherwise, keep the selector removed so pods can schedule anywhere.
- Consider adding liveness/readiness probes to the Deployment to ensure stable rollouts and endpoint health.
- Keep avoiding the :latest tag for reproducibility; we’re using a pinned version.

Want me to add health probes or restore the app to listen on port 80 instead (and switch Service targetPort back to 80)?

Steps.message:
kubectl top isn’t available (metrics API missing), so I pulled usage directly from the kubelet Summary API.

Master/control-plane nodes found:
- mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane (Ready, role=control-plane, capacity: 4 CPU, ~15.6 GiB RAM)

Current resource usage (instantaneous):
- CPU: ~0.097 cores used (~2.4% of 4 cores)
- Memory:
  - Working set: ~1.11 GiB (~7% of 15.6 GiB)
  - Total (incl. cache/buffers): ~3.73 GiB (~24%)
- Disk (root fs): ~62.4 GiB used of ~144.2 GiB (~43% used)
- Network since boot: RX ~677 MiB, TX ~10.2 MiB
- Swap: 0 used (3.0 GiB available)

Top control-plane pod consumers (instantaneous):
- CPU: kube-apiserver ~0.036 cores; etcd ~0.020 cores; kube-controller-manager ~0.011 cores
- Memory working set: kube-apiserver ~337 MiB; ingress-nginx ~65 MiB; kube-controller-manager ~65 MiB; etcd ~54 MiB

If you want kubectl top to work, install the Metrics Server (e.g., kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml).

- Disk (root fs): ~62.4 GiB used of ~144.2 GiB (~43% used)
- Network since boot: RX ~677 MiB, TX ~10.2 MiB
- Swap: 0 used (3.0 GiB available)

Top control-plane pod consumers (instantaneous):
- CPU: kube-apiserver ~0.036 cores; etcd ~0.020 cores; kube-controller-manager ~0.011 cores
- Memory working set: kube-apiserver ~337 MiB; ingress-nginx ~65 MiB; kube-controller-manager ~65 MiB; etcd ~54 MiB

If you want kubectl top to work, install the Metrics Server (e.g., kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml).

Additional details about resource usage are extra but non-contradictory and acceptable per the criterion.

results.taskOutput:
Done. I resized the volume for storage-pod to 3Gi in the resize-pv namespace by recreating its PVC:

- storage-pvc now requests 3Gi.
- This PVC was unbound, so recreating it was safe and avoided data loss.
- The StorageClass uses rancher.io/local-path with WaitForFirstConsumer; provisioning will occur once the pod is scheduled.

Current status:
- PVC storage-pvc: Pending (waiting for first consumer/provisioner)
- Pod storage-pod: Pending (will schedule once the volume is provisioned)

Say the word if you want me to monitor until the PVC binds and the pod is running, or if you’d like me to verify the final capacity inside the pod once it comes up.

outputSteps.content:
Done. I updated the Deployment rollout-test/web-app to use image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.25 and kept a rolling update strategy that ensures no downtime.

What I did:
- Updated the Deployment image to 1.25.
- Preserved rolling strategy with maxUnavailable: 25% and maxSurge: 25%. With 3 replicas this yields:
  - maxUnavailable -> 0 (25% of 3 floors to 0) so no downtime
  - maxSurge -> 1 (25% of 3 rounds up to 1) allowing one extra pod during the rollout

Verification:
- Rollout completed: Deployment web-app observedGeneration 2, updatedReplicas 3, readyReplicas 3, availableReplicas 3.
- New ReplicaSet web-app-7db4956fd is serving 3/3 pods, all Running.
- Events show a smooth scale-up of the new RS and scale-down of the old RS with no warnings.

If you want me to lock in zero disruption even if replicas change in the future, I can explicitly set maxUnavailable: 0 and an appropriate maxSurge (e.g., 1) in the spec.

Below is a single, ready-to-apply multi-document YAML that creates the requested namespaces, RBAC, service accounts, resource quotas, and network policies according to the requirements (least-privilege, isolation, default-deny, DNS access). Apply with: kubectl apply -f <this-file>. If your CoreDNS uses a non-standard label, adjust the k8s-app: kube-dns selector in the allow-dns-egress policies.

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-alice
  labels:
    role: developer
    tenant: alice
    environment: dev
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: dev-bob
  labels:
    role: developer
    tenant: bob
    environment: dev
---
apiVersion: v1

    role: developer
    tenant: charlie
    environment: dev
---
apiVersion: v1

  name: dev-shared
  labels:
    role: shared
    environment: dev
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: staging
  labels:
    role: staging
    environment: staging
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: prod
  labels:
    role: prod
    environment: prod

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: owner-admin
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1

metadata:
  name: owner-admin
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob

    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1

  name: owner-admin
  namespace: dev-charlie

  - kind: User

  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---

  name: developers-view
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-standard
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:

    requests.memory: "4Gi"

---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-ingress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Ingress"]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-egress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Egress"]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Egress"]
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              k8s-app: kube-dns
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace-ingress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Ingress"]
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace-egress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Egress"]
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: ["Egress"]
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              role: shared

- Default-deny policies are applied to all non-system namespaces; kube-system is untouched. DNS egress is explicitly allowed to CoreDNS (k8s-app=kube-dns).

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I set up:
- Namespace: statefulset-test
- Headless Service: db (for stable identity)
- StatefulSet: db with 3 replicas
  - Container: busybox:1.36 (sleep)
  - Volume: 1Gi PVC per pod, mounted at /data
  - Init container writes: echo -n "initial_data" > /data/test

Then I scaled the StatefulSet down to 1 replica.

Notes and how to verify:
- The remaining pod should be db-0. The PVCs are retained, so data for db-1 and db-2 stays on their claims (data-db-1, data-db-2).
- Verify pods: kubectl get pods -n statefulset-test
- Verify data on db-0: kubectl exec -n statefulset-test db-0 -- cat /data/test
- Verify PVCs: kubectl get pvc -n statefulset-test

If your cluster doesn’t have a default StorageClass, the PVCs will stay Pending; let me know if you want me to specify a StorageClass.

Steps.message:
Done.

What I set up:
- Namespace: statefulset-test
- Headless Service: db (for stable identity)
- StatefulSet: db with 3 replicas
  - Container: busybox:1.36 (sleep)
  - Volume: 1Gi PVC per pod, mounted at /data
  - Init container writes: echo -n "initial_data" > /data/test

Then I scaled the StatefulSet down to 1 replica.

Notes and how to verify:
- The remaining pod should be db-0. The PVCs are retained, so data for db-1 and db-2 stays on their claims (data-db-1, data-db-2).
- Verify pods: kubectl get pods -n statefulset-test
- Verify data on db-0: kubectl exec -n statefulset-test db-0 -- cat /data/test
- Verify PVCs: kubectl get pvc -n statefulset-test

If your cluster doesn’t have a default StorageClass, the PVCs will stay Pending; let me know if you want me to specify a StorageClass.

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What I set up:
- Namespace: statefulset-test
- Headless Service: db (for stable identity)
- StatefulSet: db with 3 replicas
  - Container: busybox:1.36 (sleep)
  - Volume: 1Gi PVC per pod, mounted at /data
  - Init container writes: echo -n "initial_data" > /data/test

Then I scaled the StatefulSet down to 1 replica.

Notes and how to verify:
- The remaining pod should be db-0. The PVCs are retained, so data for db-1 and db-2 stays on their claims (data-db-1, data-db-2).
- Verify pods: kubectl get pods -n statefulset-test
- Verify data on db-0: kubectl exec -n statefulset-test db-0 -- cat /data/test
- Verify PVCs: kubectl get pvc -n statefulset-test

If your cluster doesn’t have a default StorageClass, the PVCs will stay Pending; let me know if you want me to specify a StorageClass.
